coredata.dc:description: Solid-state CsPbBr3-based perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and an enhanced stability were synthesized using a convenient centrifugal casting method combined with silica encapsulation and Na-doping techniques. The optical properties, composition features, surface morphology and stability of the PNC films were investigated in detail. By utilizing this centrifugation method, CsPbBr3@SiO2 PNC films with an extraordinarily uniform surface were obtained with a high PLQY of 58.0%. Furthermore, the PLQY of CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films was improved to a large value of 74.7% after Na + -ion doping of PNCs encapsulated with silica. At the same time, both the CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films, especially the latter, showed substantially improved material stability against heat, ultraviolet (UV) light and environmental conditions. LED devices fabricated using the obtained PNC films exhibited excellent optical and color properties, demonstrating the great application potential for solid-state PNC films prepared here in the light-emitting and display fields.

Optical absorption and PL emission spectra for CsPbBr3 films (a), CsPbBr3@SiO2 films (b) and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films (c); (d) PL decay curves for CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films.
High-resolution XPS spectra for Cs 3d (a), Br 3d (b) and Pb 4f (c) in CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films.

The fitting parameters for the PL decay curves, the average lifetime (τaver), the PL quantum yield (PLQY), the radiative transition rate KR, the nonradiative transition rate KNR and the KR/KNR ratio for the three PNC films.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of perovskite nanocrystal films with a high luminous efficiency and an enhanced stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state CsPbBr3-based perovskite nanocrystal (PNC) films with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and an enhanced stability were synthesized using a convenient centrifugal casting method combined with silica encapsulation and Na-doping techniques. The optical properties, composition features, surface morphology and stability of the PNC films were investigated in detail. By utilizing this centrifugation method, CsPbBr3@SiO2 PNC films with an extraordinarily uniform surface were obtained with a high PLQY of 58.0%. Furthermore, the PLQY of CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films was improved to a large value of 74.7% after Na + -ion doping of PNCs encapsulated with silica. At the same time, both the CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films, especially the latter, showed substantially improved material stability against heat, ultraviolet (UV) light and environmental conditions. LED devices fabricated using the obtained PNC films exhibited excellent optical and color properties, demonstrating the great application potential for solid-state PNC films prepared here in the light-emitting and display fields.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, all-inorganic lead halide (CsPbX3, X = Cl, Br and I) perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs) have attracted significant attention due to their excellent photoelectric properties, including a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) for PL emission, a readily tunable band gap and a long carrier diffusion length [1–4]. Numerous efforts have been devoted to the syntheses and applications of colloidal CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) PNCs [5–8], and extraordinary progress has been achieved. However, due to their natural ionic structure, low formation energy and high surface trap-state density, PNCs suffer from poor chemical, optical and thermal stability [1,9], which seriously hinders their practical applications in various optoelectrical devices, such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs), lasers and photodetectors [10–15].

The instability of CsPbX3 PNCs mainly originates from its incomplete surface passivation and internal intrinsic defects, which depend on NC synthesis. In the conventional methods used to synthesize colloidal PNCs, the desorption of straight long-chain ligands, such as oleic acid and oleylamine, weakly bound to the surface of PNCs during the precipitation step can increase the density of surface trapping defects and destroy the crystal structure of the NCs [16,17], eventually leading to agglomeration among the nanocrystals or decomposition, particularly in polar solvents. In fact, regardless of whether long-chain or short-chain ligands are used for the preparation of PNCs, different degrees of luminescence efficiency reduction generally occur during the purification as a result of the desorption of ligands [18,19]. Meanwhile, larger crystals with a low PLQY are always discarded, and only a small number of PNCs with a high PLQY are retained in the supernatant after purification; thus, the product yield is very low [4,20]. However, for device applications it is essential to prepare solid-state perovskite materials with a high PLQY. Unfortunately, in the formation process of solid powders or films, a roll-off in the PL efficiency of colloidal NCs, even to half or one third of the solution phase, is a common phenomenon because of various surface damage during manufacturing (Table S1, Supporting Information) [21–31]. To achieve PNCs with a high PLQY, many studies have been performed using branched capping ligands, such as silane with a stronger passivation capability, to prepare smaller and more uniform PNCs due to substantial steric hindrance [19,32,33]. After the hydrolysis of silane, the strong steric hindrance and the formation of silica effectively prevent PNCs from agglomerating, which helps them preserve their structural and optical properties [19]. Consequently, encapsulation with silica has become one of the most effective strategies for maintaining luminescence and enhancing the stability of PNCs (Table S2, Supporting Information) [31,34–43]. On the other hand, more recently the strategy of heteroatom doping with various alkali metal cations (Li+, Na+, K+ and Rb+) was adopted to reduce the internal defect state density, passivate grain boundaries (GBs), and enhance the PLQY or power conversion efficiency (PCE) and stability of PNCs and films (Table S3, Supporting Information) [29,44–49]. In particular, doping with univalent Na + ions has been demonstrated to significantly improve the PLQY and the stability of zero-dimensional PNCs both theoretically and experimentally [29,49].

With the aim of achieving solid-state PNC films with a high PLQY and stability, a simple centrifugal casting method was noted for its advantages of completing the purification of colloidal PNCs with a narrow size distribution and the preparation of solid-state films in one step [21], thus avoiding damage to the PL performance of PNCs during the synthesis of the films through the use of multiple processing steps. However, Younghoon Kim et al. reported that the highest PLQY value was only 18% for PNC films prepared using this centrifugal method [21], indicating a great possibility for improvement. Recently, we developed a centrifugal method by combining silica encapsulation and Na + -ion doping techniques to deposit CsPbBr3-based PNC films and enhance their PLQY and stability at the same time. In this work, centrifugally cast CsPbBr3 PNC films encapsulated with silica achieved a rather uniform surface and a high PLQY of 58.0%. Upon doping a small amount of Na + ions into PNCs encapsulated with silica, the PL quantum efficiency of CsPbBr3 PNC films was further improved to a large value of 74.7%, which is higher than most of the PLQY values for perovskite films reported in Table S1. Moreover, our work enhancing the PL performance of PNC films is still ongoing. Compared with CsPbBr3 PNC films without silica encapsulation and Na + -doping, both the silica-encapsulated films and Na + -doped/silica-encapsulated films exhibited a substantially improved material stability against heat, ultraviolet (UV) light and environmental conditions (especially the latter films, which showed a better stability). In optoelectrical applications, the obtained high-efficiency PNC films used as the fluorescence conversion layer in LEDs showed a strong optical conversion capability. Furthermore, white LEDs manufactured using PNC films and commercial nitride red phosphor exhibited excellent photochromic properties with color coordinates of (0.33,0.34), a temperature of 5555 K, a rendering index of 63.1, and a power efficiency of 52.8 lm/W. The successful achievement of high-efficiency and high-stability PNC films indicates that the improved centrifugal casting method used here is effective at preserving the high luminescence of PNCs and enhancing the material stability, which is of great significance in pushing forward applications of solid-state PNC materials in optoelectrical devices.

CsPbBr3-based PNC films were synthesized by using the modified centrifugal casting method, with the synthesis diagram shown in Scheme 1 . First, the CsPbBr3 precursor solution was prepared under ambient conditions at room temperature. To prepare the precursor solution, CsBr and PbBr2 were chosen as the Cs and Pb sources, respectively, in order to avoid the introduction of undesired impurities, and all reagents were analytically pure. CsBr (0.8 mmol) and PbBr2 (0.8 mmol) were dissolved in one pot with 20 ml of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OLA) were simultaneously added at a volume ratio of DMF:OA:OLA = 10:1:0.15 to promote the dissolution of the lead source and keep the solution stable. The precursor solution was stirred for 20 min until the yellow precipitates were completely dissolved. To prepare the silica-encapsulated CsPbBr3 PNC films, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) was used as the capping ligand and silica precursor. After the precursor solution (1 ml) and different volumes of the APTES/DMF solution (0.5 mol/L) were mixed to obtain solutions with different Si:Pb atomic ratios, the mixed solution (100 μl) was added to toluene (5 ml) in order to separate the CsPbBr3 PNCs capped by APTES (the lower left inset of Scheme 1) according to the supersaturation and precipitation principle [50]. For the substrate material, commercially available quartz glass was thoroughly cleaned before being placed into the above CsPbBr3 PNC solution. Subsequently, a centrifugal process was performed at a low rotating speed of 4000 rpm for 5 min to cast CsPbBr3 PNCs capped by APTES into solid-state films onto glass substrates (the lower right inset of Scheme 1). Finally, after the cast films were removed from the solution and naturally dried under ambient conditions, CsPbBr3@SiO2 PNC films were obtained, which showed a bright blue–green emission under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light. To perform Na+-doping and silica-coating on PNCs at the same time, NaBr was chosen as the Na source, and three types of solutions, i.e., the CsPbBr3 precursor solution, NaBr/DMF solution (0.1 mol/L) and an APTES/DMF solution (0.5 mol/L), were mixed at a certain Si:Pb:Na atomic ratio. Then, a solution mixture (100 μl) with three types of solutions was added to toluene (5 ml) to obtain the Na+-doped CsPbBr3 PNCs simultaneously capped by APTES. Finally, CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films were cast on the glass substrates via centrifugation under the same conditions as for the CsPbBr3@SiO2 PNC films. For comparison, neither the Na+-doped nor silica-coated CsPbBr3 PNC films were prepared using this centrifugal casting method by directly adding the precursor solution (100 μl) to toluene (5 ml) in order to separate the CsPbBr3 PNCs capped by OA and OLA. To fabricate the LED or white LED devices, a naked 460 nm blue chip was chosen as the light source, and the green or green/red fluorescence conversion layers on the chip were covered to form the LEDs or white LEDs. The green layers were composed of CsPbBr3@SiO2 or CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films, while the red layers were manufactured via spin-coating onto quartz glass and heating at 100 °C for 15 min after 0.2 g of commercial nitride red phosphor (610 nm, Ray Power Optoelectronics (Suzhou) Co. LTD) was mixed uniformly with silicone resin A (1.5 g) and silicone resin B (1.5 g).

The PL spectra for the CsPbBr3-based PNC films were characterized with an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). A UH4150 UV–Vis–near infrared spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was then used to record the absorption spectra of the PNC films. The time-resolved PL spectra were measured using an FS5 fluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were measured on a Nicolet iN10 Fourier transform micro infrared imaging spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the PNC solutions were obtained by using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). SEM 450/660 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (FEI, USA) was used to observe the mapping images for the films. The surface roughness of the PNC films was characterized using a Dimension ICON atomic force microscope (AFM) (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured under CuKα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA) with an Ultima IV diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). By adjusting the concentration of the CsPbBr3-based PNCs in the toluene solution, the film thickness can be controlled; the film thickness was determined through SEM cross-section observations to be between a few microns to tens of microns.

In studies of PNCs, it is well known that the film-forming ability of NCs with a small size and a high luminescence efficiency is always difficult because the accumulation of NCs in films can readily result in an agglomeration effect and reduce the quantum efficiency. In this work, we determined that the film-forming ability of PNCs can be conveniently realized during the preparation of CsPbBr3@SiO2 PNC films via the centrifugal method, as shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). Fig. S1 shows photos of the APTES-capped PNC solution in the centrifuge tube before and after centrifugation under no UV light and UV light irradiation. Before centrifugation, as shown in Fig. S1(a), APTES-capped CsPbBr3 PNCs were dispersed homogeneously in toluene solution, and no NC precipitates appeared at the bottom of the centrifuge tube under the low NC concentration. Under UV-light irradiation, the above NC solution emitted uniform blue–green light, as shown in Fig. S1(b). At this moment, the glass substrate had been put into the solution, but the substrate was so transparent that it could not be observed whether under UV-light irradiation or not. After centrifugation, most PNCs were observed to preferentially deposit onto the surface of quartz glass, meaning a higher use ratio of NCs, but there were still a small amount of extremely small PNCs in the supernatant at a middling centrifugal speed of approximately 4000 rpm here, while few precipitates were found at the bottom of the centrifuge tube, as shown in Fig. S1(c). When UV light irradiated the centrifuged solution, strong blue–green light was emitted from PNC films on glass substrates, and the emission of the solution was weaker than that of the films, as shown in Fig. S1(d). Based on the above observations, the mechanism behind this preferential adhesion of PNCs capped by APTES on glass possibly originates from the binding of APTES with glass through the condensation reaction of Si-OH groups to form Si-O-Si bonds during hydrolysis [36], which will be confirmed by the FTIR results in the text below because the Si-OH group is an intermediate product of APTES hydrolysis and abundant on the clean glass surface. Meanwhile, inside the PNC films, the hydrolysis of APTES capping layers around PNCs would lead to the formation of a cross-linked organic silica network, which has been verified in many studies [36,51–53], probably playing a positive role in the formation of PNC films with a micron grade thickness on the substrates here. The specific formation mechanism of PNC films needs further study. On the other hand, the adhesion between CsPbBr3 PNC films and glass substrates was observed to be weak in the experiment. Regarding CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 PNC films, it was determined that the films easily broke up and desorbed from the glass substrates upon doping with a larger NaBr dose, which could disturb the linkage of the silica network. These observations also confirm the effect of the APTES ligand on the film-forming ability of PNCs.

First, the photoluminescence properties of three types of PNC films, i.e., CsPbBr3, CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, were investigated in detail, as shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) shows the PL emission spectra for the CsPbBr3 films (Si:Pb = 0:1), CsPbBr3@SiO2 films with different Si:Pb molar ratios and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films with different Na:Pb and Si:Pb ratios of 1:1. As shown in Fig. 1(a), CsPbBr3 films exhibit a green emission located at 520 nm, while all the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films show shorter emission wavelengths than the CsPbBr3 films. For the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films, their emission colors change from green (517 nm) to blue (470 nm) with an increasing Si:Pb ratio from 0.375:1 to 2:1. The blueshift of the PL emission peaks for the above two types of PNC films is supposed to result from the size-dependent effect of the PNCs [19,51], meaning that the shorter the wavelength is, the smaller the PNC size. In CsPbBr3 films, the larger crystal size is due to the aggregation effect of PNCs as a result of the desorption of OA and OLA ligands from the PNC surface during the centrifugal process. For CsPbBr3@SiO2 films, the coordinating effect of the capping ligands will lead to a slower delivery rate for monomers with an increased number of APTES capping ligands [19]; thus, the size of the PNCs decreases with an increasing Si:Pb ratio. As far as CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films are concerned, as shown in Fig. 1(a) one can see that their emission wavelengths near 513 nm remain almost unchanged, compared with the 514 nm emission from CsPbBr3@SiO2 films with the same Si:Pb ratio of 1:1, upon incorporating a small amount of Na+ ions with Na:Pb ratios of 0.125:1 and 0.25:1, which indicates that Na doping here has little effect on the PNC size distribution. Fig. 1(b) displays the variation in the PLQY for the film samples shown in Fig. 1(a). It can be observed that CsPbBr3 films capped by OA and OLA have a lower PLQY of 12.5%, while the PLQY for CsPbBr3@SiO2 films increases with an increasing Si:Pb ratio and reaches a maximum value of 58.0% at Si:Pb = 1:1; then, the PLQY decreases as the Si:Pb ratio continues to increase. Based on the above experimental observations, compared with the CsPbBr3 films, the reduced size and enhanced PLQY of PNCs in CsPbBr3@SiO2 films clearly demonstrate the successful replacement of straight-chain capping ligands (OA and OLA) by branched-chain APTES with a stronger capping and passivation ability on the PNC surface. Additionally, the PLQY variation trend for CsPbBr3@SiO2 films indicates that an insufficient ligand brings about inadequate passivation leading to a lower PLQY, while an excessive ligand will lead to a greater steric hindrance, which results in a smaller NC size and more uncoordinated surface atoms, thus reducing the PLQY [19]. Therefore, under an optimized Si:Pb ratio of 1:1, Na doping was performed to prepare the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, which exhibited a continuously improved PLQY with a maximum value of 74.7%. The outstanding PLQY of the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films demonstrates the effectiveness of the univalent Na + ions in enhancing the emission efficiency of PNCs, which is consistent with previous reports [29,49]. The inset in Fig. 1(b) shows photos of the CsPbBr3 films and CsPbBr3@SiO2 films illuminated using 365 nm ultraviolet light, which confirm the variation in the emission wavelength and PLQY for the two types of PNC films shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b), while CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films emit brighter and more dazzling light than CsPbBr3@SiO2 films with Si:Pb = 1:1 (pictures are not shown here).

In the following studies, CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films (Si:Pb = 1:1) and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films (Si:Pb = 1:1, Na:Pb = 0.25:1) were chosen as representative samples to systematically investigate their UV–visible absorption spectra, PL decay curves, XPS features, surface morphology and stability. As shown in Fig. 2 , the absorption and emission spectra for the three films were contrasted, and their PL decay curves were displayed. As shown in Fig. 2(a), steep excitonic absorption was observed in the wavelength range from 490 nm to 525 nm for the CsPbBr3 films. Both CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films exhibit similar absorption edges, as shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c), which show a slight blueshift compared with CsPbBr3 films, corresponding to a blueshift of their PL emission peaks from 520 nm to 513 nm. By comparing the absorption and PL emission spectra for three PNC films, we found that both the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films show a Stokes shift of approximately 17 nm (comparable to CsPbBr3 films (15 nm)), which suggests that the emission characteristics are related to direct exciton recombination and a stronger energy conversion capability from absorption to emission [4]. To explore the mechanism behind the high PLQY observed for the obtained PNC films, the PL decay curves for the three films were measured, as shown in Fig. 2 (d). All decay curves were found to be well fitted with a biexponential decay function, and the fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 . Based on the fitted short lifetime (τ1) and long lifetime (τ2), in Table 1 the average lifetime (τaver) can be calculated according to Eq. (1) [54]:

From Eq. (5) it can be seen that PLQY has no positive or negative dependence on the lifetime, but PLQY is always proportional to KR/KNR. For the CsPbBr3, CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, the obtained KR/KNR values are 0.143, 1.381, and 2.954, respectively. The enhanced KR/KNR ratio for the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films compared with that for the CsPbBr3 films demonstrates that the encapsulation of PNCs with silica can raise the radiative recombination proportion (KR/KNR) above 1 by effectively passivating the surface defects through the branched-chain ligand, which is generally considered to be the source of the nonradiative transition. In the case of CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, the KR/KNR ratio is further increased to 2.954, which confirms that the strategy of combining surface passivation with ion doping inside PNCs can remarkably improve the radiative recombination proportion of PNC films compared to only surface passivation and encapsulation processing, possibly due to the nature of PNCs containing more internal defects.

XPS measurements were performed for three representative film samples to determine their compositions and chemical bonding, as shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) and Fig. 3 . Compared with the XPS survey spectra for the CsPbBr3 films in Fig. S2 (a), an Si 2p peak at 101.6 eV appears, and an O 1s peak at 530.6 eV becomes stronger in the XPS survey spectra for CsPbBr3@SiO2 films in Fig. S2(b). These two peaks correspond to the binding energies of SiO2, which indicates the formation of Si-O-Si bonds [56,57]. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S2(b), the N 1s peak at 401.1 eV is also observed and originates from charged N species, suggesting interactions with the PNC surface [58]. In Fig. S2(c), in addition to the weaker signals for Si 2p and N 1s and a stronger peak for O 1s, another stronger peak for Na 1s at 1070.6 eV was also detected in CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, as shown in the inset of Fig. S2(c), demonstrating the presence of Na + cations. In CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, the Cs/Pb/Br/Na/Si/O/N atomic ratio (%) was determined to be 1.2:1.7:4.9:19.1:10.1:47.1:15.9 via XPS measurements. Noticeably, the Na:Pb ratio is 19.1:1.7, which is much higher than the nominal doping amount of 0.25:1. Simultaneously, the Na:Pb ratio is also much higher than 0.02:1 at the nominal value of 1.25:1 according to the recent literature [49]. The extraordinarily high doping amount of Na + ions detected here is considered to be related to the capping layer of APTES around the PNCs with substantial steric hindrance shown in the lower left inset of Scheme 1, which not only prevents PNCs from agglomerating, but also limits Na+ ions from dissolving into the solution again. In addition, the existence of a large amount of Na+ ions indicates a great driving force for the incorporation of Na+ ions inside the crystal lattice of the PNCs, which significantly eliminates the internal defects, accounting for the enhanced radiative recombination and PLQY, as shown in Table 1 and Fig. 1(b). Fig. 3(a), (b) and (c) show the XPS high-resolution spectra obtained for the three PNC films involving Cs 3d, Br 3d and Pb 4f, respectively. In Fig. 3(a) and (b), the core level peaks for Cs 3d and Br 3d have similar shapes and positions for the three PNC films, while the core level peaks for both Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 shift toward higher binding energies for CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The increased binding energies for the Pb2+ ions indicate that the chemical environment of the Pb2+ ions is modified by capping encapsulation and Na doping, confirming the successful replacement of the surface ligand APTES and the effective Na doping. To further explore the surface functional groups of PNCs, FTIR spectra for three types of PNC solutions and films were measured, as shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information). Fig. S3(a) gives the FTIR spectra for three types of PNC solutions, i.e., the CsPbBr3 solution, APTES-capped CsPbBr3 solution and Na-doped/APTES-capped CsPbBr3 solution, before centrifugation. In Fig. S3(a), the CsPbBr3 solution shows a broad O-H stretching band from 3300 to 3600 cm-1, resulting from OA [59], while the stronger O-H bands for the APTES-capped CsPbBr3 solution and Na-doped/APTES-capped CsPbBr3 solution indicate the successful capping and hydrolysis of APTES, which is consistent with previous reports [51,60]. After centrifugation, in the obtained CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, the O-H stretching bands become much stronger than those of the CsPbBr3 films, suggesting the existence and hydrolysis of APTES onto the surface of PNCs, as shown in Fig. S3(b). Noticeably, both Si-OH bonds at 930 cm-1 and Si-O-Si modalities at 790, 1031 and 1117 cm-1 were observed in the CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, confirming the formation of a cross-linked organic silica network through the condensation reaction of silicon-hydroxyl groups (Si-OH) [36,51–53], which coincides with the XPS results in Fig. S2.

The surface morphologies of the three PNC films were observed via SEM, as shown in Fig. 4 (b), (d) and (f), corresponding to CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, respectively. For a comparison, Fig. 4 (a), (c) and (e) show TEM and HR-TEM images of three CsPbBr3 PNCs, i.e., NCs capped by OA and OLA, NCs capped by APTES and Na-doped NCs capped by APTES, respectively, in a toluene solution before casting the corresponding PNC films using the centrifugal method. As shown in Fig. 4(a), CsPbBr3 NCs capped by OA and OLA have a square shape with a large size range of 10–25 nm and an average size of 13.1 nm, as shown in the lower left inset of Fig. 4(a). The HR-TEM image shown in the upper right inset displays a spacing of 0.58 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal plane, demonstrating the cubic structure of these NCs. After the CsPbBr3 NCs were cast into solid-state films, as shown in Fig. 4(b), the obtained CsPbBr3 films exhibited larger grain sizes of 10–50 nm, which is consistent with the longer emission wavelength and lower PLQY shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b), indicating that the aggregation effect of NCs is unavoidable during centrifugation due to the separation of OA and OLA from the NC surface. In addition, some irregular holes were observed on the surface of the CsPbBr3 films, implying a noncompact structure for the films, possibly leading to a poor stability. As shown in Fig. 4(c), CsPbBr3 NCs capped by APTES exhibit smaller grain sizes of 5–10 nm with an average size of 9.0 nm, as shown in the lower left inset of Fig. 4(c). The upper right inset of Fig. 4(c) also shows a cubic (110) spacing of 0.41 nm. Noticeably, the centrifugally cast CsPbBr3@SiO2 films show a uniform surface and dense structure, which is comprised of a large number of small particles approximately 10–20 nm in size, as shown in Fig. 4(d), which is in accordance with the smaller emission wavelength and the higher PLQY shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b). Fig. 4(e) shows the TEM and HR-TEM images of Na-doped CsPbBr3 NCs capped by APTES, which clearly exhibit square particles in a 5–10 nm size range with an average size of 8.1 nm, as shown in the lower left inset of Fig. 4(e), and a 0.29 nm spacing for the cubic (200) crystal plane. As shown in Fig. 4(f), the corresponding CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films show a denser internal structure and an uneven surface, thus the grains are hard to distinguish on the surface of the films, suggesting that the accumulation of the NaBr dopant in the grain boundary of the PNC films is consistent with the large amount of Na+ ions detected by the XPS measurement in Fig. S2(c). Additionally, the PLQYs for the above three PNC solutions were measured to be 43.0%, 66.0% and 76.6% for NCs capped by OA and OLA, NCs capped by APTES and Na-doped NCs capped by APTES, respectively. Compared with the PLQYs for the three corresponding PNC films, i.e., 12.5%, 58.0% and 74.7% shown in Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that both silica encapsulation and Na doping indeed helped preserve the PL efficiency of the PNCs. The XRD characteristics of the three PNC films also confirm the same cubic structure as that of NCs in solutions, as shown in Fig. S4, although a wide diffraction peak due to amorphous silica appears and almost submerges the signals due to the PNCs [38,61]. In this study, AFM measurements were performed to evaluate the surface roughness of three PNC films, as shown in Fig. S5. The roughness values of the CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films were determined to be 51.6 nm, 15.5 nm and 44.7 nm, respectively, which are consistent with the above SEM observations of the three PNC films. In addition, the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 film with an Na:Pb ratio of 0.125:1 has a roughness of 22.0 nm, as determined by AFM. From the roughness results, it can be seen that Na doping brings roughness damage to PNC films, which will be improved by coordinating the Na:Pb:Si ratio and further optimizing the experimental conditions in later work.

Based on the above results, it can be deduced that the size, structure, and PL performance of PNCs in solid-state films can be well maintained after centrifugal processing with the aid of silica encapsulation and Na doping. Therefore, the obtained PNC films are expected to show an enhanced stability, which is more advantageous for actual optoelectronic applications. In this study, the thermal and light stability of three PNC films were investigated in detail, as shown in Fig. 5 . To evaluate their thermal stability, the influence of the thermal cycling times on the PL emission of three PNC films was researched over five thermal cycles by heating the samples from 20 °C to 100 °C and then cooling them from 100 °C to 20 °C. Fig. 5(a) plots the remaining PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 films, CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films at every temperature point during five cycles. As shown in Fig. 5(a), one can clearly see that the remaining PL emission intensity of all samples decreases with an increasing temperature during the heating process and then increases with temperature upon cooling. For comparison purposes, the remaining PL intensity values for the three films are illustrated at the end of every thermal cycle, as shown in Fig. 5(b). When five thermal cycles are finished, the PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 films is reduced to approximately 10% of the initial value, and the PL intensity of the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films drops to 51%, while the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films still preserve their relative emission intensity of 77%. The unrecoverable PL intensity for the three films indicates the generation of nonradiative recombination centers that are thermally activated during the thermal cycles, which results in degradation of the PL performance [49]. On the other hand, compared with CsPbBr3 films, the improved thermal stability of CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films demonstrates the significance of encapsulation with silica and Na doping in enhancing the capability of PNCs against thermal attacks. The light stability of the three PNC films was explored by irradiating the samples with a 365 nm UV light source in a darkroom with a UV analyzer in ambient air at room temperature, with obvious differences found for the three films, as shown in Fig. 5(c). From Fig. 5(c), one can see that the PL intensity of the CsPbBr3 films drops sharply and is completely quenched after only 30 days, while the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films still maintain 40% of the initial PL intensity until 90 days. It is satisfactory that CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films retain almost the original PL emission for at least 90 days, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The remarkably improved stability of the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films might be attributed to Na doping in the lattice and the PNC grain boundaries, which is consistent with the above XPS and SEM results shown in Fig. S2(c) and Fig. 4(f). In fact, studies of the UV light illumination stability of PNC films also involve the storage stability of samples in a natural environment. Based on the above analyses of the thermal and light illumination stability of the PNC films, we can conclude that PNC films with a high luminous efficiency and a high stability were achieved by using the convenient centrifugal casting technique.

To examine the application potential of the PNC films in the optoelectronic light-emitting and display field, an LED device was fabricated by covering a naked blue chip with CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films that showed the highest PL efficiency, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 6 (a). The light spectrum for the above LED device shown in Fig. 6(a) shows the 513 nm blue–green emission for the CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films, which is much stronger than the 450 nm blue emission from the chip, undoubtedly demonstrating the high light-emitting efficiency of the PNC films. Furthermore, a white LED device was assembled by successively overlapping the CsPbBr3@SiO2 films and a commercial red phosphor layer by spin-coating directly onto the blue chip, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6(b). Fig. 6(b) shows the typical emission spectrum for the white LED device fabricated here, which shows three discrete emission peaks located at 450 nm, 513 nm and 610 nm, corresponding to the blue LED chip, the blue–green PNC films and the red phosphor layer, respectively. In the photo shown in Fig. 6(b), the white LED working at a 3 mA current shows a bright white light with excellent optical and color properties, i.e., CIE color coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), a color temperature of 5555 K, a rendering index of 63.1 and a high power efficiency of 52.8 lm/W, which are as great as those achieved recently using silica-coated perovskite quantum dot/polystyrene (SPQD/PS) films and CdSe QD/silica monoliths [51,62]. The stability of the white LED device obtained here during use was examined by continuously lighting it up with a working current of 3 mA in ambient air. The experimental results show that the EL spectra can remain stable even after continuous work for 72 h, as shown in Fig. S6. The dependence of the white LED emission spectrum on the driving current is also displayed in Fig. 6(c), where the emission intensity from the PNC films increases continuously when the driving current is increased from 10 mA to 80 mA, indicating that the PL performance of the PNC films does not show an obvious saturation phenomenon under blue light. Furthermore, the optical and color parameters can be improved in the future by better matching the fluorescent conversion layers and reducing the light scattering.

In summary, in this study a centrifugal casting method was used to prepare solid-state PNC films. To improve the PLQY and stability of PNCs, a strategy based on combining silica encapsulation with Na doping was adopted to overcome the agglomeration problem of NCs, reduce the internal defects and passivate the grain boundaries. The results showed that the nanocrystal size, structure and PL performance of PNCs in solid-state films can be well maintained compared with the PNC solution after centrifugal processing with the aid of silica encapsulation and Na doping. Using this approach, uniform CsPbBr3@SiO2 films with a higher PLQY were cast, probably relying on the linkage of the branched APTES capping layers around PNCs during the hydrolysis process. Upon doping Na + ions into PNCs encapsulated with silica, the PL efficiency of CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films can be further improved upon. Additionally, CsPbBr3@SiO2 and CsPbBr3@Na@SiO2 films exhibit substantially enhanced material stability. The application potential of the solid-state PNC films obtained here was demonstrated by fabricating LED devices with excellent optical and color properties. The approach and strategy used here can provide technical enlightenment for achieving solid-state PNC materials with high PLQYs and stability in order to advance their applications in optoelectrical devices.
